title: How do you validate that a binary tree is a BST?
answer: Recurse with a **(min, max) range** for each node, narrowing it as you descend left (tighten max) and right (tighten min); a node violating its range fails. Equivalently, an **inorder traversal** of a valid BST is strictly increasing. O(n).

title: How do you find the maximum depth of a binary tree?
answer: Recurse: depth of a node = `1 + max(depth(left), depth(right))`, with null nodes contributing 0. It visits each node once — O(n) time, O(h) stack space. A BFS level count gives the same answer iteratively.

title: How do you compute the diameter of a binary tree?
answer: The diameter is the longest path between any two nodes. For each node, the candidate is `leftHeight + rightHeight`; recurse returning heights while tracking the global max. O(n), computing height and diameter in one pass to avoid O(n²).

title: How do you check if a binary tree is symmetric?
answer: Compare the tree against its **mirror**: recursively check that `left.left` matches `right.right` and `left.right` matches `right.left`, with values equal at each step. O(n) time. It can also be done iteratively with a queue of node pairs.

title: How do you serialize and deserialize a binary tree?
answer: Do a **preorder traversal** emitting node values and a sentinel (e.g. `#`) for nulls into a string. To deserialize, consume tokens in the same preorder, building nodes and stopping branches at sentinels. Both are O(n). BFS/level-order encoding works too.

title: How do you find the kth smallest element in a BST?
answer: An **inorder traversal** of a BST yields sorted order, so the kth visited node is the answer — stop early once you've counted k. Average O(h + k) using an explicit stack. Augmenting nodes with subtree sizes makes repeated queries O(h).

title: How do you check if a root-to-leaf path sums to a target?
answer: Recurse, **subtracting** each node's value from the target. At a leaf, success means the remaining target equals the leaf's value (i.e. reaches 0). Explore left or right; any successful branch returns true. O(n).

title: How do you build a binary tree from preorder and inorder traversals?
answer: The **first preorder element is the root**; find it in the inorder array to split left/right subtrees, then recurse. Use a hashmap of inorder indices and a moving preorder pointer for O(n) instead of O(n²) searching.

title: How do you get the right-side view of a binary tree?
answer: Do a **level-order BFS** and take the **last node of each level**, which is what you'd see from the right. Alternatively a DFS visiting right-first and recording the first node seen at each new depth. O(n).

title: How do you clone a graph?
answer: Traverse with **BFS or DFS**, keeping a hash map from original node → its clone. When visiting an edge, create the neighbor's clone if unseen, then link clones. The map handles cycles and shared nodes. O(V+E).

title: How do you solve the course schedule (prerequisites) problem?
answer: Model courses as a directed graph of prerequisites; a valid schedule exists iff there's **no cycle**. Use **Kahn's algorithm** (repeatedly remove zero in-degree nodes) — if you can't remove all of them, there's a cycle. O(V+E).

title: How do you count connected components in an undirected graph?
answer: Run **DFS/BFS** from each unvisited node, marking everything reachable, and increment a counter per launch — each launch is one component. O(V+E). **Union-Find** gives the same count as the number of distinct roots after unioning all edges.

title: How do you solve the word ladder problem?
answer: Treat each word as a node with edges to words differing by **one letter**; the shortest transformation is **BFS** from start to end. Generate neighbors by trying every position × every letter, checking the word set. Bidirectional BFS speeds it up.

title: How do you check if a graph is bipartite?
answer: Try to **2-color** the graph with BFS/DFS: assign a node one color and its neighbors the opposite. If you ever need to give two adjacent nodes the same color, it isn't bipartite (it has an odd cycle). O(V+E).

title: How does the Bellman-Ford algorithm work?
answer: **Bellman-Ford** relaxes all edges V−1 times to find shortest paths from a source, handling **negative weights** (unlike Dijkstra). A V-th relaxation that still improves a distance reveals a **negative cycle**. Runs in O(V·E).

title: What is the difference between Kruskal's and Prim's MST algorithms?
answer: Both build a **minimum spanning tree**. **Kruskal's** sorts edges and adds the cheapest that doesn't form a cycle (using Union-Find) — great for sparse graphs, O(E log E). **Prim's** grows a tree from one node, always adding the cheapest boundary edge via a heap — O(E log V), better for dense graphs.

title: How do you merge k sorted lists?
answer: Put the head of each list into a **min-heap**, repeatedly pop the smallest and push its successor — O(N log k) for N total elements. Alternatively merge lists pairwise (divide and conquer) for the same complexity without a heap.

title: How do you find the median from a data stream?
answer: Keep a **max-heap** of the lower half and a **min-heap** of the upper half, balanced in size. The median is the top of one heap (odd count) or the average of both tops (even). Insert is O(log n); median query is O(1).

title: How do you find the k closest points to the origin?
answer: Keep a **max-heap of size k** by squared distance (no need for sqrt); push each point and pop the farthest when size exceeds k. O(n log k). Quickselect on distances gives average O(n) when a single batch result is enough.

title: How do you find the minimum number of meeting rooms required?
answer: Sort meetings by start time and use a **min-heap of end times**; for each meeting, if the earliest end ≤ its start, reuse that room (pop), else allocate a new one (push). The heap's max size is the answer. O(n log n).

title: How do you solve the 0/1 knapsack problem?
answer: Use **DP** where `dp[w]` is the best value for capacity w. For each item, iterate capacities **downward** (so each item is used once), taking `max(dp[w], dp[w - weight] + value)`. O(n × capacity) time, O(capacity) space.

title: How do you find the longest increasing subsequence?
answer: The O(n²) DP sets `dp[i]` = longest LIS ending at i. The optimal O(n log n) approach keeps a **tails array** of the smallest possible tail for each length, binary-searching where each element fits — the array's length is the LIS length.

title: How do you compute the edit distance between two strings?
answer: **DP** table where `dp[i][j]` is the edit distance of prefixes. If characters match, carry `dp[i-1][j-1]`; else `1 + min` of insert, delete, replace. O(m×n) time; space can be reduced to O(min(m,n)) with rolling rows.

title: How do you solve the house robber problem?
answer: You can't rob two adjacent houses, so `dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i])`. Track just two running variables for O(n) time, O(1) space. The circular variant runs it twice, excluding the first or last house.

title: How do you solve the word break problem?
answer: `dp[i]` is true if the prefix of length i can be segmented into dictionary words. For each i, check every split j where `dp[j]` is true and `s[j..i]` is in the dictionary. O(n²) with a word set; a trie can speed up substring checks.

title: How do you count unique paths in a grid?
answer: Moving only right or down, the number of paths to a cell is `paths(up) + paths(left)`, with edges initialized to 1. Fill the grid bottom-up in O(m×n), reducible to O(n) with a single row. Closed form: `C(m+n-2, m-1)`.

title: How do you check if an array can be partitioned into two equal-sum subsets?
answer: If the total is odd, it's impossible. Otherwise it reduces to a **subset-sum / 0-1 knapsack**: can any subset reach total/2? Use a boolean `dp[sum]` updated downward per element. O(n × sum) time, O(sum) space.

title: How do you find the maximum product subarray?
answer: Because negatives flip signs, track both the **max and min product** ending at each index (a min can become the max after multiplying by a negative). Update both each step and keep the global max. O(n) time, O(1) space.

title: How does Quickselect find the kth smallest/largest element?
answer: **Quickselect** partitions around a pivot like quicksort but **recurses into only one side** — the side containing the kth index. Average O(n), worst O(n²) with bad pivots (mitigated by random or median-of-medians pivots). It avoids fully sorting.

title: What is a monotonic stack and when do you use it?
answer: A **monotonic stack** keeps elements in increasing or decreasing order, popping violators as you push. It solves 'next/previous greater or smaller element' problems — like daily temperatures or stock spans — in O(n), since each element is pushed and popped once.

title: What is binary search on the answer?
description: Explain binary searching a value range.
answer: When the answer is monotonic — a candidate value either works or doesn't, with a clean threshold — you **binary search over the value range** instead of an array, using a feasibility check at each midpoint. Used for problems like Koko eating bananas or minimum ship capacity. O(log(range) × checkCost).
